---
title: "About"
toc: false
about:
  template: trestles
  image: foto3.jpeg
  links:
    - icon: linkedin
      text: LinkedIn
      href: https://www.linkedin.com/in/joao-vieira-iseg/
    - icon: envelope
      text: Email
      href: mailto:joaoviei7a@gmail.com
    - icon: github
      text: GitHub
      href: https://github.com/joaovieira22
---

I'm an economist and data scientist working at the intersection of
**causal inference**, **pricing**, and **forecasting**.

My day-to-day work is about turning a business question into an
identification strategy, a model, and a defensible number. I care less
about which method is fashionable this year and more about which one
actually answers the question in front of me — and about being honest
when the data can't.

I'm currently a Senior Consultant at **EY-Parthenon** in Lisbon, working
on econometric evaluation and decision analytics for public and private
stakeholders. In parallel, I'm finishing a **Ph.D. in Economics** at
**ISEG – University of Lisbon**, on nonlinear panel-data estimators with
heterogeneous effects. Before EY I spent three years as a Visiting
Assistant Professor at ISEG, teaching applied econometrics, microeconomics
and investment evaluation.

This blog is where I think out loud about the methods I use. Some posts
are theory and intuition. Others are full walkthroughs with R code you
can run. Most sit in between. The goal is rigour without textbook padding.

## What I write about

- **Causal inference** — DiD, RDD, IV, synthetic control, DML, causal
  forests. Identification strategies, DAGs, potential outcomes, and the
  robustness checks that keep you honest.
- **Pricing & demand** — price elasticity, demand modelling, two-sided
  market pricing, dynamic pricing, MMM, causal price experiments.
- **Experimentation** — A/B testing under interference, switchback
  experiments, CUPED, uplift modelling, heterogeneous treatment effects.
- **Forecasting** — probabilistic forecasting, model evaluation,
  uncertainty quantification, and where forecasting stops being causal
  inference.

## Background

- **Ph.D. in Economics** — ISEG, University of Lisbon *(2020 – present)*
- **MSc in Applied Econometrics and Forecasting** — ISEG, University of Lisbon
- **BSc in Management** — Faculty of Economics, University of Coimbra
- **Senior Consultant** — EY-Parthenon *(2024 – present)*
- **Visiting Assistant Professor** — ISEG, University of Lisbon *(2021 – 2024)*

## Affiliations

- Member, **REM** — Research in Economics and Mathematics
- Member, **CEMAPRE** — Center for Applied Mathematics for Forecasting
  and Economic Decision

*All views are my own. Code for every post is available on
https://github.com/joaovieira22.*
